MIKE ROOS 
ASSEMBLYMAN, FORTY_SIXTH DISTRICT 
January 6, 1978 
Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Re: 
Request for opinion persuant to Section 38ll4B 
Dear Mr. Lowenstein: 
COMM!TTFES 
CRiMiNAL JUSTICE 
HOUSING AND 
COM\lTUNITY DEVELOPMENT 
SUBCOM M tTTEE 
LAW ENFORCEMENT 
SPECiALIZED TRAIN,NG 
After conversation with Mr. Lee Rosenthal of your office I 
would like a written opinion persuant to Section 38ll4B of the 
Act in regard to the use of a computer and terminal in my office. 
We have been exploring the possibility of using computer 
time and a terminal with acoustic coupler for constituent list 
compilation and maintenance, information retrieval, casework 
simplification and other office related functions. 
The terminal is portable and therefore makes it versatile 
in its use. 
Being portable, we would like the option of using the 
terminal and computer time for campaign purposes. 
To use it for 
campaign purposes would require moving the terminal to a site not 
paid for by the state. 
In addition, to identify whether operation 
is for state or campaign use, we would institute the operation of 
two separate computer time code numbers for billing. 
Therefore, when 
being used for state purposes the state billing number would be used 
and when in operation for campaign use, the campaign number would 
be used. 
All computer software, approximately $500.00 for program 
development, would be paid for by the campaign. 
This sofeware 
would be for both state and campaign use. 
The one time installment 
cost, approximately $70.00 would be paid for by the campaign. 
The 
monthly terminal rental cost could be paid for in two ways: 
one-
by splitting the cost evenly between the state and campaign (using 
~" 


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein 
January 6, 1978 
Page 2 
part of the $800.00 assembly contingent for the state portion); 
two - by checking the use and splitting the cost accordingly. 
The actual computer time, billed monthly, would be registered 
by the amount of time used. 
I think the implementation of such a system is not only 
efficient and practical but will lead to the most effective use 
of employees time in serving our constituents. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter. 
MR:

sections.question: In your letter, you described the possible use of a computer and terminal for both state and campaign use. The terminal, when used for campaign purposes, would be moved to a location not paid for by the state. The cost of software (approximately $500) would be paid for by the campaign. The one time installment cost (approximately $70) would be paid for by the campaign. The monthly terminal rental cost could be paid for in two ways: one - by splitting the cost evenly between the state and campaign; two - by checking the use and splitting the cost accordingly. The actual computer time, billed monthly, would be registered by the amount of time used.
sections.conclusion: The staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission finds nothing in your proposal which conflicts with the provisions of the Political Reform Act. Of course, the payments made by the campaign committee for the computer, rental, equipment, software, and monthly billing must be reported as expenditures on the campaign's campaign statements. The staff does recommend that the monthly rental be paid by splitting the cost on the basis of the actual state and campaign use.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a California state assemblyman use a shared computer terminal and system for both state office and campaign purposes, with costs allocated between the state and campaign committee?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC found no conflict with the Political Reform Act for the proposed shared computer use arrangement. Campaign payments for the system must be properly reported as campaign expenditures, and FPPC recommends splitting rental costs based on actual state versus campaign usage.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (January 6, 1978) and FPPC response letter (January 17, 1978). Some text is degraded/illegible in the original document, particularly in the middle section of the response letter where specific cost allocations are discussed. Extracted the clearest and most complete passages from both portions of the document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In your letter, you described the possible use of a computer and terminal for both state and campaign use. The terminal, when used for campaign purposes, would be moved to a location not paid for by the state. The cost of software (approximately $500) would be paid for by the campaign. The one time installment cost (approximately $70) would be paid for by the campaign. The monthly terminal rental cost could be paid for in two ways: one - by splitting the cost evenly between the state and campaign; two - by checking the use and splitting the cost accordingly. The actual computer time, billed monthly, would be registered by the amount of time used.

CONCLUSION: The staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission finds nothing in your proposal which conflicts with the provisions of the Political Reform Act. Of course, the payments made by the campaign committee for the computer, rental, equipment, software, and monthly billing must be reported as expenditures on the campaign's campaign statements. The staff does recommend that the monthly rental be paid by splitting the cost on the basis of the actual state and campaign use.

embedding.summary: Assemblyman Mike Roos requested FPPC advice on using a portable computer terminal for both state office and campaign purposes, with costs shared between the state and campaign. FPPC approved the arrangement with the requirement that campaign-related costs be properly reported and recommended allocating rental costs based on actual usage.
